title: Egypt & Nubia
subtitle: Three thousand years along the Nile.
intro: Pharaonic Egypt and its southern rival Kush left monuments and portraits of startling precision. The pyramids alone span a thousand years — and Nubia built more of them than Egypt did.

works.title: Great Pyramid of Giza
works.desc: Tomb of Khufu. 2.3 million stone blocks, averaging 2.5 tons each. The tallest human-made structure on earth for 3,800 years.

works.title: Great Sphinx of Giza
works.desc: Carved from a single limestone outcrop, the oldest known monumental sculpture. Its nose was already missing before Napoleon arrived.

works.title: Narmer Palette
works.desc: A ceremonial siltstone palette showing a king smiting an enemy — one of the earliest monuments of unified Egypt, with its conventions already fully formed.

works.title: Bust of Nefertiti
works.desc: Painted limestone, found in the workshop of the sculptor Thutmose at Amarna. Still in Berlin; Egypt has asked for it back for a century.

works.title: Mask of Tutankhamun
works.desc: Eleven kilograms of solid gold, inlaid with lapis, carnelian, and obsidian. The funerary mask of a boy-king who died at nineteen.

works.title: Karnak Temple Complex
works.desc: A city of temples at Thebes, built and rebuilt by thirty dynasties. The hypostyle hall alone has 134 columns.

works.title: Temple of Hatshepsut
works.meta: New Kingdom · c. 1470 BCE · Deir el-Bahari
works.desc: Three stacked colonnaded terraces built into a cliff for Egypt's longest-reigning female pharaoh. Her successor tried to erase her — and failed.

works.title: Tomb of Nefertari
works.meta: 19th dynasty · c. 1255 BCE · Valley of the Queens
works.desc: Painted for Ramesses II's favorite wife — the queen walks through the underworld, plays the board game Senet, and greets the gods. Colors still vivid.

works.title: Abu Simbel
works.meta: Reign of Ramesses II · c. 1264 BCE
works.desc: Four colossal statues guard the temple front. In the 1960s the whole complex was cut apart and moved 200 feet up to escape Lake Nasser.

works.title: Rosetta Stone
works.desc: A decree written three times — in hieroglyphs, Demotic, and Greek — that became the key to reading Egyptian. Champollion cracked it in 1822.

works.title: Pyramids of Meroë
works.meta: Kingdom of Kush · 300 BCE – 300 CE · Sudan
works.desc: Over 200 pyramids, steeper and smaller than Egypt's, built for Nubian royalty. Sudan has more pyramids than Egypt — about twice as many.

works.title: Book of the Dead
works.desc: Spells written on papyrus to guide the deceased through the Duat. The Weighing of the Heart against a feather is its most famous scene.

works.title: Colossi of Memnon
works.desc: Twin seated statues of Amenhotep III, sixty feet tall. After an earthquake cracked one, it "sang" at dawn for two centuries — until Romans repaired it.

works.title: Fayum Mummy Portraits
works.desc: Encaustic panels placed over the faces of mummies. The most personal, most realistic portraits to survive from the ancient world — they still look you in the eye.

works.title: Temple of Dendur
works.desc: A small sandstone temple given to the United States in 1965 when Lake Nasser was about to drown it. Reassembled stone by stone inside the Met.

works.title: Pyramid of Djoser
works.desc: The first pyramid in Egypt — six stacked mastabas designed by the architect Imhotep, later deified as a god of wisdom and medicine.

works.title: Luxor Temple
works.meta: New Kingdom · Amenhotep III / Ramesses II · c. 1400–1250 BCE
works.desc: Every year during the Opet Festival, statues of the gods were carried in boats from Karnak to here. A Roman camp, Christian church, and mosque all followed.

works.title: Temple of Edfu
works.desc: The best-preserved temple in Egypt, because it was buried in desert sand for centuries. Dedicated to Horus, with reliefs of his battle against Seth.

works.title: Tomb of Seti I
works.meta: 19th Dynasty · c. 1279 BCE · Valley of the Kings
works.desc: The longest, deepest, and most richly painted tomb in the valley. Every wall is covered in funerary texts; ceilings map the stars.

works.title: Colossal Statue of Akhenaten
works.desc: The heretic pharaoh depicted with a long face, wide hips, and swollen belly — a deliberate break from centuries of idealized royal portraiture.

works.title: Ramesseum
works.meta: New Kingdom · Ramesses II · 13th c. BCE · Thebes
works.desc: Memorial temple of Ramesses II. A collapsed colossus here inspired Shelley's "Ozymandias" — though Shelley never saw it.

works.title: Medinet Habu
works.meta: New Kingdom · Ramesses III · c. 1150 BCE · Thebes
works.desc: The fortress-temple of the last great warrior pharaoh. Its walls narrate the repulse of the Sea Peoples — history preserved because the victor carved it.

works.title: Dendera Temple
works.desc: Temple of Hathor. Its astronomical ceilings include the only known circular zodiac from ancient Egypt — now a replica; the original is in the Louvre.

works.title: Kom Ombo
works.desc: A rare double temple — one half for the crocodile god Sobek, the other for Horus the Elder. Mummified crocodiles still fill a side chamber.

works.title: Tomb of Tutankhamun
works.meta: 18th Dynasty · c. 1323 BCE · Valley of the Kings
works.desc: A small, hastily decorated tomb, overlooked by thieves for three thousand years. Howard Carter opened it in 1922 and the world went Egyptomaniac again.

works.title: The Seated Scribe
works.desc: Painted limestone, eyes inlaid with crystal and copper. Alert and ready, reed in hand — the realist portrait of a bureaucrat, 4,500 years ago.

works.title: Khafre Enthroned
works.desc: Diorite portrait of the pharaoh who built the second pyramid. Horus the falcon wraps his wings around the back of the king's head — divine kingship locked in stone.

works.title: Pyramid of Meidum
works.desc: A failed smooth-sided pyramid whose outer casing collapsed. What survives is the blunt tower at the core — an architectural experiment that taught the builders of Giza.

works.title: Sphinx of Taharqa
works.desc: A Nubian pharaoh in the form of an Egyptian sphinx, carrying a double crown he had earned as conqueror. Now in the British Museum.

works.title: Great Sphinx of Tanis
works.meta: Old to Middle Kingdom · c. 2600–1800 BCE
works.desc: One of the largest sphinxes outside Giza — reused and re-inscribed by pharaoh after pharaoh. Now guards the entrance of the Louvre's Egyptian galleries.

works.title: Mastaba of Ti
works.desc: The tomb of a minor royal official, painted inside with daily-life scenes — farming, fishing, ships under sail — and regarded as the high point of Old Kingdom relief.

works.title: Dendera Zodiac
works.desc: A circular sandstone relief of the night sky carried off from the ceiling of Dendera temple by a French explorer in 1821. Now in the Louvre; a replica hangs in the original.

works.title: Prisse Papyrus
works.desc: The oldest literary book in existence. Two teachings of wisdom — "the Instructions of Kagemni" and "of Ptahhotep" — carefully written in cursive hieroglyphs.

works.title: Reliefs from the Tomb of Seti I
works.meta: c. 1290 BCE · Painted limestone · Sully Wing
works.desc: The goddess Hathor welcomes Seti I into the afterlife. Cut from the walls of KV17 in the Valley of the Kings in the 19th century — a practice we now consider vandalism, but which saved this relief from the flooding that ravaged the tomb.

works.title: Stele of King Djet (The Serpent King)
works.desc: Over 5,000 years old — from the very dawn of writing. A serpent (the king's name) stands above the serekh, the palace façade, topped by Horus the falcon. This is what Egyptian writing looked like before it became hieroglyphs.

works.title: Amarna Letters
works.desc: Clay tablets of diplomatic correspondence between Egypt and its neighbors — Babylonia, Assyria, the Hittites, Canaanite city-states. Written in Akkadian, the lingua franca of the Bronze Age.

works.title: Bent Pyramid
works.meta: Old Kingdom · Sneferu · c. 2600 BCE · Dahshur
works.desc: Sneferu's second pyramid — the angle changes partway up, either to prevent collapse or because ambition had to meet physics. A crucial step between stepped and smooth pyramids.

works.title: Temple of Seti I at Abydos
works.desc: An L-shaped temple with seven chapels. Its Abydos King List carved on one corridor became a key scholarly tool — naming 76 pharaohs back to the First Dynasty.

works.title: Beni Hasan
works.desc: Thirty-nine rock-cut tombs of provincial governors, walls painted with daily life — wrestling matches, bakers, looms, dancers — a rare survival of Middle Kingdom domestic art.

works.title: Merneptah Stele
works.desc: Black granite stele listing the pharaoh's victories. Line 27 contains the earliest known mention of "Israel" in any historical record.

works.title: Red Pyramid
works.meta: Old Kingdom · Sneferu · c. 2575 BCE · Dahshur
works.desc: Sneferu's third pyramid — the first true smooth-sided pyramid to work. Named for the reddish limestone exposed after the original white casing was stripped for later buildings.

works.title: Valley of the Kings
works.meta: New Kingdom · c. 1550–1070 BCE · West Bank, Thebes
works.desc: Sixty-five painted tombs cut into the limestone valley. The pharaohs chose it for its isolation — looters found everything anyway, except Tutankhamun.

works.title: White Chapel of Senusret I
works.desc: A small bark chapel of fine white limestone. Torn down as filler in a later pylon; found reassembled piece by piece and reconstructed in the 1930s.

works.title: Younger Memnon (Ramesses II)
works.desc: A granite colossus of Ramesses II. Belzoni dragged it out of the Ramesseum in 1816 and shipped it to the British Museum. Shelley saw it — and wrote Ozymandias.

works.title: Colossal Statue of Ramesses II
works.meta: New Kingdom · c. 1250 BCE · Grand Egyptian Museum
works.desc: Eleven meters of carved granite, 83 tons. Moved from downtown Cairo to the Grand Egyptian Museum in a 2006 road spectacle so large they had to raise bridges.

works.title: Gayer-Anderson Cat
works.desc: A hollow-cast bronze cat in the form of the goddess Bastet, with gold earrings and nose ring. Slightly battered but uncannily alive — one of the most reproduced pieces in the British Museum.

works.title: Deir el-Medina
works.desc: The workers' village for those who built the royal tombs. Its own tombs are painted with a precision surprising for common craftsmen — because the craftsmen were the painters of the Valley of the Kings.

works.title: Temple of Hibis
works.meta: Late Period · 6th c. BCE · Kharga Oasis
works.desc: The largest surviving temple of the Persian period in Egypt. Carved in sandstone in the middle of a desert oasis; its roof blocks depict 700 Egyptian deities.

works.title: Memphis (Egypt)
works.meta: Early Dynastic onward · c. 3100 BCE · Lower Egypt
works.desc: The first capital of unified Egypt, sacred to Ptah. What survives today is ruins in a palm grove — a colossal reclining statue of Ramesses II, sphinxes, embalmed Apis bulls.

works.title: Temple of Amun at Jebel Barkal
works.desc: Built by Thutmose III below a sandstone butte the Nubians believed Amun lived inside. Reused by Kushite pharaohs who conquered Egypt from here in the 25th Dynasty.

works.title: Palermo Stone
works.meta: Old Kingdom royal annals · c. 2400 BCE · Egypt
works.desc: A black basalt slab inscribed with the reigns, heights of Nile floods, and major rituals of every king from the predynastic era through Dynasty V. Only seven fragments survive of what must have been a two-meter-tall master chronology.

works.title: Seated Statue of Hemiunu
works.desc: A life-size limestone portrait of the vizier who is thought to have overseen construction of Khufu's Great Pyramid. His ample stomach and heavy jowls were a deliberate display of elite wealth — the opposite of the idealized kingly physique.

works.title: Models from the Tomb of Meketre
works.desc: Dozens of painted wooden miniatures — a cattle count, weaving workshops, porticoed villas, fishing boats — sealed in a hidden chamber beside Meketre's tomb. Discovered intact in 1920, they provide the most detailed snapshot of Middle Kingdom daily life.

works.title: Statue of Mentuhotep II
works.meta: Dynasty XI · c. 2030 BCE · Deir el-Bahari
works.desc: The unifier of Egypt after the First Intermediate Period, painted with the black skin of Osiris and wearing the red crown of Lower Egypt. His deliberately massive, almost archaic body announces a new Middle Kingdom style grounded in reborn Upper Egyptian traditions.

works.title: Tomb-Chapel of Nebamun
works.desc: Painted fragments from a scribe's vanished tomb, including the famous scene of Nebamun fowling in a marsh with a spotted cat retrieving birds. The butterflies in flight are rendered with a freedom the Egyptian grid rarely allowed.

works.title: Pyramid of Unas
works.desc: A modest pyramid whose burial chamber is inscribed from floor to ceiling with the Pyramid Texts — the earliest religious writings known. Blue-painted hieroglyphs cover 283 spells guiding the king's ascent to the imperishable stars.

works.title: Pyramid Texts
works.desc: The oldest religious corpus on earth, carved into the walls of royal burial chambers at Saqqara. Written in Old Egyptian and painted turquoise, the spells teach the dead king how to fly to the sky, evade demons, and rejoin his father the sun.

works.title: Avenue of Sphinxes
works.meta: New Kingdom–Ptolemaic · c. 1380 BCE onward · Luxor
works.desc: A 2.7-kilometer processional road flanked by more than 1,300 ram-headed and human-headed sphinxes connecting Luxor Temple to Karnak. Reopened in 2021 after twenty years of excavation stripped away centuries of silt and medieval housing.

works.title: Great Hypostyle Hall of Karnak
works.desc: A forest of 134 lotus-bud and papyrus-bloom columns covering half a hectare, the tallest reaching 21 meters. Seti I and Ramesses II finished its decoration; inside, the sandstone sky was painted a dense blue and dotted with yellow stars.

works.title: Reserve Heads
works.desc: Limestone heads placed in Old Kingdom tombs as substitute bodies in case the mummy was destroyed. Their ears are deliberately chipped off or their necks grooved — probably a ritual 'killing' to prevent the spirit from wandering back into the land of the living.

works.title: Pyramids of El-Kurru
works.desc: The earliest royal cemetery of the Kushite 25th Dynasty, where the pharaohs who briefly ruled Egypt lie beneath small, steep-sided pyramids. Inside, painted chambers fuse Egyptian afterlife iconography with distinctly African imagery of hunting and cattle.

works.title: Pyramids of Nuri
works.desc: The burial field of Taharqa, last of the Kushite Black Pharaohs, and twenty of his successors. Recent underwater excavations have plunged archaeologists into flooded burial chambers still partially filled with gilded coffin ornaments and shawabtis.

works.title: Gebel Barkal
works.meta: Kushite sacred mountain · c. 1450 BCE onward · Sudan
works.desc: A 98-meter sandstone butte the Kushites believed housed the creator-god Amun. The New Kingdom pharaohs built a temple at its base and the Napatan kings inscribed a pinnacle-shaped outcrop with gilded reliefs now almost vanished to weather.

works.title: Kerma
works.desc: Capital of the first kingdom of Nubia, whose massive mud-brick Western Deffufa still rises 18 meters. The royal tumuli concealed astonishing human sacrifices — hundreds of courtiers buried alive around their king to serve him in the afterlife.

works.title: Tomb of Tutankhamun Interior (KV62)
works.meta: Dynasty XVIII · c. 1323 BCE · Valley of the Kings
works.desc: Yellow walls densely painted with the funerary Opening of the Mouth rite and Tutankhamun being greeted by Osiris. Oddly cramped for a king, the tomb was almost certainly borrowed after his unexpected death at about nineteen.

works.title: Serapeum of Saqqara
works.meta: New Kingdom to Ptolemaic · c. 1300 BCE onward · Saqqara
works.desc: Underground vaults housing the mummies of the sacred Apis bulls, each sealed in a granite sarcophagus weighing up to seventy tonnes. How the Egyptians maneuvered them down the narrow subterranean corridors is still debated.

works.title: Canopic Jars
works.meta: New Kingdom onward · c. 1500 BCE – 1000 BCE
works.desc: Four jars holding the mummy's liver, lungs, stomach, and intestines. Their lids shaped as the four sons of Horus — jackal, baboon, falcon, and human — each guarding a separate organ.

works.title: Rahotep and Nofret
works.desc: Painted limestone seated pair from Meidum. Rock-crystal eyes so convincing that workmen at the excavation are said to have fled. Among the earliest masterpieces of Egyptian portraiture.

works.title: Triad of Menkaure
works.desc: Pharaoh Menkaure flanked by the goddess Hathor and a nome personification. One of a set from his Giza valley temple — divine companionship made into state sculpture.

works.title: Meidum Geese
works.desc: A painted frieze of six geese from Nefermaat's mastaba chapel — species recognizably rendered, colors preserved like yesterday's paint. One of the freshest-feeling images from 4,600 years ago.

works.title: Head of Senusret III
works.desc: A careworn face cut from hard stone — heavy eyelids, downturned mouth. The first royal portraits that reads as an aging man rather than a god, and the Middle Kingdom's break with Old Kingdom idealism.

works.title: Amarna Princess
works.desc: A small red quartzite head of a young princess from Akhenaten's new capital. The elongated skull and naturalistic features are pure Amarna — the brief revolution in Egyptian form that died with its king.

works.title: Gebel el-Arak Knife
works.desc: Flint blade with an ivory handle carved in Mesopotamian style — lions, bulls, a robed "master of animals." Evidence of Uruk-period trade reaching the Nile before Egypt was Egypt.

works.title: Battlefield Palette
works.desc: A ceremonial cosmetic palette carved with naked dead enemies being picked over by crows and lions. The earliest surviving Egyptian battlefield scene; kingship first imaged as total victory.

works.title: Scorpion Macehead
works.desc: A limestone mace showing King Scorpion cutting a ceremonial canal. One of the earliest images of an Egyptian ruler — before the country was even unified.

works.title: Two-Dog Palette
works.desc: A schist palette carved with two tall dogs gripping its edges, a wild hunt scene between them. The prey — gazelle, hare, even a flute-playing fox — bleed into chaos.

works.title: Tomb of Sennedjem
works.desc: A painted tomb at Deir el-Medina for a craftsman of the royal necropolis — its vaulted ceiling covered in the Field of Reeds, Osiris, and the afterworld's ploughing harvests.

works.title: Tomb of Rekhmire
works.desc: Vizier of Thutmose III, buried in Sheikh Abd el-Qurna. The tomb walls are a painted bureaucracy — foreign tribute, brickmaking, granary counts. One of the fullest social portraits of the New Kingdom.

works.title: Catacombs of Kom el Shoqafa
works.desc: Three levels of rock-cut tombs where Egyptian gods, Roman legionaries, and Hellenistic motifs stand on the same walls. Alexandria's last great pagan monument, discovered in 1900 when a donkey fell through the roof.

works.title: Apedemak Temple at Naqa
works.desc: A Kushite temple to the lion-god Apedemak. The reliefs show the king and queen in a distinctly African body type, inscriptions in Meroitic — a script still not fully deciphered.

works.title: Musawwarat es-Sufra
works.desc: A sprawling Meroitic temple complex in open desert — elephants carved on column bases, ramps wide enough for live ones to climb. A Kushite religious center whose original rituals are still largely mysterious.

works.title: Shabaka Stone
works.desc: A Nubian pharaoh ordered an ancient Memphite theology copied onto this granite slab because the papyrus original was crumbling. Centuries later, someone used the stone as a millstone, grinding away most of the text.

works.title: Statue of Ranofer
works.desc: High Priest of Ptah at Memphis, carved standing in limestone with painted black wig and white kilt. One of the finest Old Kingdom private statues — an Egyptian bureaucrat granted the permanence of a god.

works.title: Tomb of Nakht
works.desc: A small painted tomb for a granary scribe and astronomer. The banquet scene — three women musicians, a blind harpist, guests at table — is one of the most reproduced images from Thebes.

works.title: Funerary Mask of Psusennes I
works.desc: Gold burial mask of a pharaoh found intact at Tanis in 1940 — one of the only royal Egyptian tombs ever found unlooted. The find was buried in war headlines and nearly forgotten until much later.

works.title: William the Hippopotamus
works.desc: A small blue-green faience hippo, legs deliberately broken before burial to keep it from attacking the dead in the afterlife. Painted with lotuses; the unofficial mascot of the Met.

works.title: Ivory Statuette of Khufu
works.desc: Three inches tall — the only confirmed surviving image of the pharaoh who built the Great Pyramid. Found by Flinders Petrie at Abydos, headless, and discovered among the dirt on a sieve.

works.title: Coptic Icon of Christ and Saint Menas
works.desc: Christ stands beside a local Egyptian martyr, his hand familiarly on Menas's shoulder — an unusual intimacy for Christian iconography. A painted wood panel from Bawit monastery.

works.title: Victory Stele of Piye
works.desc: A granite stele recording the Nubian king Piye's conquest of Egypt, written in formal Egyptian. His battle narrative is unusually vivid — detailed reprimands to his generals, pardons for vanquished princes.

works.title: Taweret Statuette
works.desc: A blue faience figure of Taweret, the hippo-headed goddess who protected mothers and newborns. She stands on her hind legs, swollen-bellied, holding the protective sa-sign.

works.title: Hatshepsut Cowroid Seal
works.desc: A tiny steatite seal-amulet inscribed for "God's Wife of Amun, Hatshepsut" — before she crowned herself pharaoh. Held like a signet, pressed into clay to mark her possessions.

works.title: Seated Queen (Second Intermediate)
works.desc: The upper half of a black granite queen, broken at the waist. Neither her name nor the king she sat beside survives; her features are finely Kushite, from a murky century when Egypt's south broke away.

works.title: Head of Mentuhotep III
works.desc: A painted sandstone head, eyes rimmed with cosmetic black — possibly the shadowy eleventh-dynasty pharaoh whose reign pivoted Egypt from Thebes back to a unified state.

works.title: Kneeling Statue of Hatshepsut
works.desc: A large granite statue of Hatshepsut kneeling to offer libation jars — dressed as king, with male royal regalia, beard attached. The female pharaoh as she wished to be remembered.

works.title: Hatshepsut Ointment Jar
works.desc: A translucent travertine jar inscribed for Hatshepsut — ointment jars like this accompanied royal tombs in the hundreds. The jar still holds residue of the original scented fat.

works.title: Lion Subduing a Nubian
works.desc: A cosmetic-spoon handle of glazed blue and gold — a lion (Egypt) pinning a tied Nubian captive. Rhetoric of imperial power shrunk down to toiletry.

works.title: Purification of Queen Kiya
works.desc: A painted limestone relief fragment — attendants pour libation over a royal woman, probably Kiya, Akhenaten's secondary queen. Her name was later chiseled out in an Amarna damnatio memoriae.

works.title: Striding Statue of Minnefer
works.desc: A painted limestone standing figure of a royal nobleman, left foot advanced in the canonical Egyptian stride. One of the most complete private statues from Giza's west field.

works.title: Head of Userkaf
works.desc: A greywacke head of the pharaoh who founded the 5th Dynasty and built the first sun temple. The crown is lost; the drilled pupils still look directly at you.

works.title: Headrest of Irut
works.desc: A small alabaster headrest from a tomb — Egyptians slept with wooden or stone blocks under their necks in life and in death. Irut's name is inscribed in the base.

works.title: Amenhotep III in the Blue Crown
works.desc: A granite head of Amenhotep III wearing the khepresh — the leather-studded battle-crown usually worn for war. The father of Akhenaten rules at Egypt's peak.

works.title: Talatat of Nefertiti
works.desc: A small sandstone block from Akhenaten's dismantled Karnak temple — a tightly cropped profile of Nefertiti, already in the exaggerated Amarna style. 45,000 talatat blocks have been recovered.

works.title: Caryatid Mirror
works.desc: A bronze mirror whose handle is a cast nude woman supporting the polished disk on her head. Mirrors were gifts for women's tombs, often with handle-figures of beauty goddesses.

works.title: Apis Bull Statue
works.desc: A striding stone Apis bull — the sacred bull of Memphis, worshipped as living god and mummified when it died. Greco-Egyptian in style, made when Egypt's rulers spoke Greek and still honored the ancient cults.

works.title: Vulture Headdress Inlay
works.desc: A tiny gold-glass inlay of a vulture headdress — a fragment from a larger votive mask. Vulture headdresses crowned goddesses like Mut and Nekhbet.

works.title: Statue of Amenemhat III
works.desc: A granite statue of the 12th Dynasty pharaoh in the brooding Middle Kingdom portrait style — heavy eyelids, set jaw. He built the twin pyramids at Dahshur and reclaimed the Fayum oasis.

works.title: Middle Kingdom Statuette of a Man
works.desc: A small striding wooden figure of a scribe or official — smaller, more private statues proliferated in the Middle Kingdom, placed in modest tombs beside big official statues.

works.title: Coffin of Senbi
works.desc: A painted wooden coffin lid from Meir, carved and painted with a detailed facade — windows, doors, decorative patterns. A Middle Kingdom coffin was a miniature house for the afterlife.

works.title: Heqat, the Frog Goddess
works.desc: A small stone frog-headed goddess — Heqat, goddess of childbirth. Even at the dawn of Egypt, gods had animal forms and midwives invoked Heqat in labor.

works.title: Bull's-Head Amulet
works.desc: A tiny stone amulet shaped as a bull's head — pierced to string around the neck. Bull imagery precedes Egypt itself; Narmer's palette is ringed by heads like this.

works.title: Predynastic Fish Palette
works.desc: A schist cosmetic palette shaped as a fish — used to grind kohl for eye makeup. Predynastic palettes began with animal shapes; a thousand years later they became the ceremonial Narmer-style plaque.

works.title: Coptic Curtain Panel with Merrymaking
works.meta: Coptic Egypt · 6th c. · linen and wool tapestry
works.desc: A tapestry-woven panel with dancers and drinkers among vines — Coptic Egypt decorated private houses with pagan-style merrymaking long after the Theodosian edicts had closed the temples.

works.title: Coptic Virgin and Child Tapestry
works.desc: A wool tapestry panel of the Virgin enthroned with Christ — one of the earliest surviving Virgin-and-Child images, woven at roughly the moment Byzantine painters were fixing the icon format at Sinai.

works.title: Heart Scarab of Nefer
works.meta: New Kingdom · Dynasty 18 · c. 1500 BCE · graywacke
works.desc: A greywacke heart scarab, placed over the mummy's chest, with a Book of the Dead spell on its underside asking the owner's heart not to testify against him at judgment.

works.title: Saqqara Relief of Nyankhnesut
works.meta: Old Kingdom · Dynasty 6 · c. 2300 BCE · painted limestone
works.desc: A tomb relief of the 6th Dynasty official Nyankhnesut seated at an offering table — the convention locked in the Old Kingdom, looking forward to three millennia of imitation.

works.title: Resist-Dyed Hanging with Biblical Scenes
works.meta: Egypt, Byzantine period · 500–550 · linen: resist-dyed, plain weave

works.title: Tunic Fragment with Segmentum
works.meta: Africa, North Africa, Egypt, Coptic-style weaver(s) · c. 500 · Wool, linen, and dye

works.title: Fragment with Satyr and Maenad
works.meta: Byzantine Empire (Egypt) · 300s CE · Linen: undyed; wool: dyed; plain weave ground with tapestry weave

works.title: Nereid (Sea-Nymph) from a Hanging
works.meta: Byzantine Empire (Egypt) · late 300s–400s CE · dyed wool, undyed linen

works.title: Clavi (Decorative Band)
works.meta: Egypt, Umayyad period (661–750) or Abbasid period (750–1258) · 700s · wool; plain weave with slit-tapestry weave

works.title: Fragment of Round Segmentum
works.meta: Africa, North Africa, Egypt, Coptic-style weaver(s) · 400s–600s CE · Wool: tabby ground, inwoven tapestry ornament

works.title: Hanging with Christian Images
works.meta: Egypt, Byzantine period · 500s · dyed wool, undyed linen: plain weave (tabby) with inwoven tapestry weave

works.title: Decorations and Sleeve from a Tunic
works.meta: Egypt, Umayyad period (661–750) or Abbasid period (750–1258) · 700s · wool; plain weave with slit-tapestry weave

works.title: Sleeve from a Tunic
works.meta: Egypt, Umayyad period (661–750) or Abbasid period (750–1258) · 700s · wool; plain weave with slit-tapestry weave

works.title: Roundel from a Curtain
works.meta: Egypt, Byzantine period · 300s CE · plain weave ground with tapestry weave and supplementary weft wrapping; undyed linen and dyed wool

works.title: Ornament from a Garment
works.meta: Egypt, Byzantine period · 400s–600s CE · Linen, undyed; wool, dyed; tapestry weave with supplementary weft wrapping

works.title: Lower Section of a Tunic
works.meta: Egypt, Byzantine period · 400s CE · Undyed linen and dyed wool: tapestry weave with supplementary weft wrapping

works.title: Relief of Agricultural Scenes
works.meta: Egypt, Saqqara, Old Kingdom (2647–2124 BCE), Dynasty 6 · c. 2311–2281 BCE · painted limestone

works.title: Relief of a King
